issues.title: Are COSE headers a greasable extension point?
issues.author: chrysn
issues.body:
The ID_CRED_x field can be a map (of COSE Header Parameters) and can fundamentally have multiple keys.

## Challenge

How *should* an EDHOC implementation behave if something spurious showed up there, eg if instead of

```
'one'
```

it said

```
{4: 'one', 12345: "narf"}
```

or instead of

```
{14: {8: ...}}
```

it said

```
{-12345: 99999999(["zort", null, [{}]]), 14: {8: ...}}
```

## Input

* [Section 3.1 of COSE](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8152#section-3.1) describes the "crit" field, indicating that generally for COSE headers, the default outside some ranges is "optional to process" -- that would indicate that indeed the greased forms should work.
* COSE allows CDDL "any" as values, which means we would also grease "any"; consequently, all the CBOR parsers inside EDHOC libraries would need to process arbitrary well-formed (and, if memory of 9528 serves, ~deterministically encoded) CBOR. Not having indeterminate length items in there helps, but still, that may be a tall order for implementations that previously got away with only processing integers and strings in that position (if they don't support KCCS).

## Outcomes

If EDHOC implementations need to ignore unknown keys, then we should grease them. (And, at least for those I've had my hands on, expediently update our implementations to actually be greasable).

If EDHOC implementations are expected to process every key, the document is fine as it is, and we may just want to summarize the discussion that lead to this conclusion in the rationale for not greasing that point.

---

Tagging @gselander who mentioned he'd think about it in the last interim this came up on.

comments.author: gselander
comments.body:
RFC 9528 says ID_CRED_x is intended to facilitate the retrieval of the authentication credential, and that applications should strive to make ID_CRED_x as unique as possible, since the recipient may otherwise have to try several keys. 

When it comes to normative text, these are the main statements:

> EDHOC MUST allow the application to read received information about credentials in ID_CRED_R and ID_CRED_I. EDHOC MUST have access to the authentication key and the authentication credential.

As I read this, ID_CRED_x is something that, when made available to the application, enables EDHOC to access the authentication key and credential. How the application does this is out of scope.

But EDHOC shouldn't pay much attention if there is yet another map element -(key, value) pair - in ID_CRED_x.

comments.author: chrysn
comments.body: Taking notes from IETF122 meeting, quoting @malishav: "there seems to be an interplay with app-profiles and grease. In my mind, greasing COSE header_map does not belong to EDHOC"

comments.author: chrysn
comments.body:
Both those feedbacks are pretty much aligned in "not our business".

How I'd process this into the document is around the lines of

> Extending the `ID_CRED_x` COSE header maps with additional items is out of scope of this document, because looking up (or extracting) a `CRED_x` from an `ID_CRED_x` is up to the application and not the EDHOC implementation. Applications are encouraged to introduce variation in `ID_CRED_x` items they send in any way that the application expects to be functional.

… maybe with an addition that

> At the time of writing, no COSE header parameters are defined for the use as GREASE, but code points for private use are available, neither in the text nor in the numeric ranges.

issues.title: Being explicit about failure from mandatory GREASE items
issues.author: chrysn
issues.body:
Should we be explicit in the consequence of applying the default processing rules for present GREASE items means to fail the exchange?

We don't currently talk about using GREASE to cause failure, and I think it's out of scope for "regular greasing" and thus this document, but they'd be handy code points for that purpose (eg. to see whether the sender of the critical GREASE can process the errors well).

comments.author: chrysn
comments.body: It is not made explicit in https://github.com/lake-wg/grease/pull/3, but the appendix points out that outside the scope of this document, there can be testing with the critical form set, and that those tests are expected to not produce working exchanges. (And will show up on users who are unsure who search for "critical" and "negative").

issues.title: Implementation report from aiocoap
issues.author: chrysn
issues.body:
I've implemented section 2.1.1 in aiocoap (which has already used GREASE in some internal tests), and run self-tests on it.

That uncovered a bug in Lakers of the very sort that actually using extension point tends to uncover (https://github.com/lake-rs/lakers/pull/416).

Some remarks back into this document:

* Section 2.1.1 does not produce items without payload – probably OK, this is not a plugtest, and generally, empty options are already in use anyway (eg. aiocoap regularly asks for credentials).
* There is no equivalent section on cipher suite greasing. 2.1.1 moved out and say something like:

  > Every time you state your cipher suite preferences (as initiator selecting it, or as responder erring and telling), roll. On 1/64, insert any GREASE suite in a random position before the last.

issues.title: Clarity on who a sender is
issues.author: chrysn
issues.body:
Meiling sent a review in https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/lake/E0tDQOVK-yItxq5i0A1vthrQrBo; this item tracks question 2.

> Question 2: Regarding who sends GREASE
>
> The current guidance seems to imply a one-way or reactive use of
> GREASE(I'm not sure. Maybe I misunderstood. The "senders" mentioned in
> the text refer to "I" or "R"?). What happens if a server only ever
> communicates with clients that don't send GREASE? Its own logic for
> handling unknown items would never be tested in the field.
>
> To ensure both sides of the connection are robust, should we more
> strongly state that both the Initiator and the Responder have a
> responsibility to proactively send GREASE, turning it into a
> bidirectional "health check"?

Actionable from this is IMO that section 2.1 can state explicitly that this can be started in any of the EDHOC messages, and thus by either party.

issues.title: Editorial fixes
issues.author: chrysn
issues.body:
Marco has submitted an extensive list of mostly editorial fixes in https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/lake/W02bEVHpBqiPb5c6R8hq2-zFuOc; this issue tracks them.

(Not listing them in detail here; I may split things out once I'm done with the parts that are just a matter of sed).

pulls.title: Add appendix on critical use
pulls.author: chrysn
pulls.body: Using EAD items as critical will cause failure – that has its uses but it's not GREASE. Pointing this out in an appendix should both help set the scope and help implementers justify that yes indeed that's good use (it just needs more context than this document can provide).

pulls.title: Address open questions
pulls.author: chrysn
pulls.body:
* Closes https://github.com/lake-wg/grease/issues/1
* Remove "does it do more than padding": Given that the Lakers implementation currently fails with some of the grease values (but not with padding), it should be apparent that there is value in greasing it this way.
